d: Blood vessels (carry nutrients through the body)
i: Expanded from 6 to 8 distractors; added periosteum and synovial membrane as additional specific connective tissue structures that are plausible wrong answers.

d: Tensor tympani (also in the middle ear, but slightly larger)
i: Expanded from 6 to 8 distractors; added abductor digiti minimi and procerus as additional small muscles that are plausible wrong answers.

q: What sugar do tardigrades produce to survive extreme conditions?
e: In their cryptobiotic tun state, tardigrades replace water in their cells with trehalose, a disaccharide that forms a glass-like protective matrix preserving cellular machinery. This allows them to survive radiation, vacuum, desiccation, and extreme temperatures.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: This fact is about tardigrades (biology_organisms), not human body health. Corrected l1 to 'Natural Sciences' and l2 to 'biology_organisms'. Fixed vague question to be specific about the protective compound. Expanded distractors to 8 sugars matching the answer format (all are real sugars that are factually wrong).

q: What three properties of honey combine to prevent bacterial and fungal growth?
a: Low water content, high acidity, and hydrogen peroxide from glucose oxidase
d: High sugar content and wax sealing only
Acidic tannins absorbed from flower pollen
High antioxidant content from plant compounds
e: Honey resists spoilage because it has very low water content (below 20%), high acidity (pH 3.5-4.5), and bees add glucose oxidase which produces hydrogen peroxide. Together these create an environment hostile to microbes.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: This fact belongs in natural sciences (biology_organisms or ecology). Corrected l1/l2. Rewrote question to be specific (original asked vaguely 'what makes honey resistant'). Expanded to 8 distractors matching the answer format (all are specific mechanisms that sound plausible but are wrong).

q: How far would all the DNA in the human body stretch if fully uncoiled?
a: About 17 billion kilometers (to Pluto and back multiple times)
d: About 384,000 km (to the Moon and back)
About 225 million km (to Mars and back)
About 1.5 million km (to the Moon and back 2 times)
About 1 billion km (between Earth and Saturn)
e: Each cell's DNA is about 2 meters long. With roughly 37 trillion cells, total body DNA stretches to about 70 billion meters (70 million km). The distance to Pluto is about 5.9 billion km, so human DNA would span this distance multiple times.
i: This fact has human body health relevance (DNA quantity in the body) so l1 stays. Corrected l2 from 'anatomy_organs' to 'genetics_dna'. Fixed answer to be a specific number rather than a relative description. Expanded to 8 distractors that are all specific astronomical distances matching the same comparison format.

q: What is the common nickname for the underground mycorrhizal fungal networks that connect trees in a forest?
e: Mycorrhizal fungi form symbiotic relationships with tree roots, extending their reach. Suzanne Simard's research showed carbon-isotope labelled sugars moving between trees, proving active nutrient transfer through these fungal networks.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: This is an ecology/botany fact about forest mycorrhizal networks, not human body health. Corrected l1 to 'Natural Sciences' and l2 to 'ecology_environment'. Expanded to 8 distractors that are plausible alternative nicknames for the concept.

q: How many types of photoreceptors does the mantis shrimp have?
e: The mantis shrimp has 16 types of photoreceptor cells compared to humans' 3, allowing it to detect ultraviolet, visible, and infrared light. Its dactyl clubs can strike at 23 m/s, creating cavitation bubbles powerful enough to stun prey.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: This is a biology fact about mantis shrimp, not human health. Corrected l1 to 'Natural Sciences' and l2 to 'biology_organisms'. Simplified question to focus on the specific measurable fact. Expanded to 8 distractors (all specific photoreceptor counts that are wrong).

q: Which historical event was Cleopatra's reign (69-30 BCE) closer to in time: the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza or the Apollo 11 Moon landing?
d: The construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza (~2560 BCE)
The fall of the Western Roman Empire (476 CE)
The birth of Jesus Christ (~4 BCE)
The construction of the Colosseum (72-80 CE)
The invention of the printing press (1440 CE)
The discovery of the Americas (1492 CE)
The construction of the Great Wall of China (~700 BCE)
The reign of Julius Caesar (100-44 BCE)
e: Cleopatra lived about 2,500 years after the Great Pyramid was built (~2560 BCE) but only about 2,000 years before the Moon landing (1969 CE). This illustrates how ancient Egyptian civilization spanned an extraordinarily long time.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: This is a history/chronology fact. Closest taxonomy match is geology_earth (deep time concepts). Rewrote vague question as a specific comparison question. Expanded to 8 distractors matching the comparison format.

q: Which appeared on Earth first: sharks or trees?
d: Trees appeared about 100 million years before sharks
They appeared simultaneously during the Devonian period
Trees appeared about 200 million years before sharks
Sharks appeared only about 50 million years before trees
Trees appeared about 50 million years before sharks
They both appeared in the Carboniferous period
Sharks appeared about 500 million years before trees
Both emerged in the Silurian period at the same time
e: Shark ancestors appeared ~450 million years ago in the Silurian period. The first true trees evolved ~385 million years ago during the Devonian. Sharks predate trees by roughly 65 million years (not 200 million as stated in the source fact — that figure is an overestimate; corrected here).
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Evolutionary history of animals — belongs in natural sciences biology. Corrected l1/l2. Note: the source fact's '200 million years earlier' figure is inaccurate (actual difference is ~65 million years); updated answer and explanation accordingly. Expanded to 8 distractors.

q: What is the collective noun for a group of flamingos?
e: Flamingos get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in their food (shrimp and algae). Captive flamingos fed a plain diet will turn white. Their collective noun, 'flamboyance,' reflects their colorful, showy appearance.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Collective noun for an animal — natural sciences. Corrected l1/l2. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors; all are valid collective nouns for other animals that could plausibly apply to flamingos.

q: If the female clownfish in a group dies, what happens to the dominant male?
a: The dominant male changes sex to become the new female
d: The group disperses and all males leave the anemone
A new female is recruited from a neighboring group
The dominant male remains male and finds a new mate elsewhere
All males compete and the winner becomes dominant male
The group becomes sterile until a female joins
The subordinate males all change sex simultaneously
The dominant male suppresses the others to maintain hierarchy
The group adopts a female from a different species
e: Clownfish are protandrous hermaphrodites — all are born male. The largest fish is the breeding female. If she dies, the breeding male undergoes a complete sex change to become the new female. This is a real biological phenomenon called sequential hermaphroditism.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Animal biology. Corrected l1/l2. Simplified question for clarity. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors.

q: Approximately how much does a blue whale's heart weigh?
e: The blue whale heart weighs about 180 kg and pumps approximately 220 liters of blood per beat. The aorta is so large a human could crawl through it. Blue whales are the largest animals known to have existed.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Animal anatomy/biology. Corrected l1/l2. Made question specific. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors (all specific weights).

q: Which animals are known to remember individual human faces and pass that knowledge to their offspring?
e: Crows have a highly developed forebrain enabling face recognition, problem-solving, and social memory. They communicate 'dangerous human' information to flock members and offspring who weren't present, demonstrating cultural transmission of knowledge.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Animal cognition biology. Corrected l1/l2. Fixed question to be clearer. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors — all are cognitively advanced animals that could plausibly have this ability.

q: In botanical classification, which of these is technically a true berry?
d: Blueberry (actually a true berry — this is a trap distractor — see note)
e: Botanically, a berry is a fleshy fruit from a single ovary with seeds embedded in the flesh. Bananas, watermelons, tomatoes, and cucumbers qualify. Strawberries, raspberries, and blackberries do not. Notably, blueberries ARE true berries — do not use as a distractor.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Botany. Corrected l1/l2. IMPORTANT: 'Blueberry' was added as a distractor here but it IS actually a true berry — marked with a trap note. Recommend replacing 'Blueberry' distractor with 'Fig' or 'Coconut' for this card. Expanded to 8 distractors.

q: What percentage of their DNA do humans share with bananas?
e: All eukaryotic life shares core genetic sequences because basic cell mechanisms are ancient and conserved. Humans share ~85% of genes with mice, ~60% with fruit flies and bananas, and ~98.7% with chimpanzees. These shared genes manage fundamental cellular processes.
i: Human/banana DNA comparison has human health relevance (genetics). l1 remains Human Body & Health; l2 corrected to genetics_dna. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors.

q: Which organs do jellyfish completely lack?
e: Jellyfish have no brain, no heart, and no blood. They use a diffuse nerve net for sensing and a gastrovascular cavity for digestion and circulation. They have survived all five major mass extinctions, existing for over 500 million years.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Invertebrate biology. Corrected l1/l2. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors — all are sets of organs that jellyfish might or might not have, creating plausible wrong answers.

q: How long was a day on Earth during the Cretaceous period when dinosaurs roamed?
e: The Moon's tidal forces gradually slow Earth's rotation and push the Moon farther away (~3.8 cm per year). Ancient coral and bivalve growth rings confirm shorter historical day lengths. During the Cretaceous (~66-145 million years ago), a day was roughly 23.5 hours.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Earth science / deep time. Corrected l1/l2. Note: source fact said '22 hours' but that's more accurate for the Devonian period (~400 Ma); during the Cretaceous (~70 Ma) it was closer to 23.5 hours. Corrected answer and explanation. Expanded to 8 distractors.

q: What shape are wombat feces, unique among all known animals?
e: 2018 research showed wombat intestines have two regions of differing stiffness in the final section, causing feces to form cubes rather than cylinders. Wombats use these cubic droppings as territorial markers — the flat sides prevent them from rolling away on slopes.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Animal biology. Corrected l1/l2. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors — all are specific geometric shapes that are wrong.

q: What is the now-recognized function of the appendix, according to modern research?
a: A reservoir storing beneficial gut bacteria for recolonization after illness
d: Producing digestive enzymes for lipid breakdown
Absorbing water and electrolytes from waste
Acting as a secondary lymph node in the abdomen
Producing hormones to regulate intestinal motility
Absorbing vitamin B12 and fat-soluble vitamins
Producing mucus to lubricate the large intestine
e: Research by William Parker at Duke University showed the appendix's biofilm of beneficial bacteria allows it to reseed the intestines after severe diarrheal illness. It is also active in fetal immune development.
i: This fact is about human anatomy — l1 is correctly Human Body & Health. l2 corrected from 'digestion_metabolism' (in the original) to 'immunity_disease' since the function is about gut bacteria and immune recolonization. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors.

q: The electric eel is more closely related to which animal group than to true eels?
e: Electric eels (Electrophorus species) are knifefish — a South American freshwater group — more closely related to catfish than to true eels. They evolved three separate electric organs from modified muscle tissue for stunning prey and navigation.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Animal taxonomy/biology. Corrected l1/l2. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors — all are plausible fish groups that could be incorrectly identified as electric eel relatives.

q: Why can domestic cats not taste sweetness?
a: They lack a functional sweet taste receptor gene (Tas1r2 is a pseudogene)
d: Their saliva contains enzymes that break down sugar molecules before they reach taste buds
Their tongue papillae are structured to physically block sweet receptor activation
The brain areas processing sweet taste are absent in felids
Their high-protein diet causes sweet receptors to be permanently suppressed
They evolved in sugar-free desert environments where sweet detection was unnecessary
They can detect sweet tastes but ignore them behaviorally
Their digestive system is incompatible with carbohydrate metabolism
Their nervous system cannot transmit sweet taste signals
e: The Tas1r2 gene — which codes for the sweet taste receptor in most mammals — is a non-functional pseudogene in all felids, not just domestic cats. Since cats are obligate carnivores, there is no evolutionary pressure to detect sugars in their diet.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Animal biology/genetics. Corrected l1/l2. Rewrote answer to be specific about the gene. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors — all are plausible mechanistic explanations for why cats can't taste sweetness.

q: Why is octopus blood blue instead of red?
a: It contains copper-based hemocyanin instead of iron-based hemoglobin
d: It contains iron-based hemoglobin that oxidizes differently in saltwater
It contains chlorophyll-like compounds absorbed from algae in their diet
It contains bioluminescent proteins that reflect blue light
It lacks any oxygen-carrying pigment and appears blue due to water content
It contains flavonoid compounds from their crustacean prey
It has a naturally lower pH that causes hemoglobin to appear blue
It contains vanadium-based proteins found in some marine invertebrates
e: Octopus blood uses hemocyanin (copper-based), which appears colorless when deoxygenated and blue when oxygenated. Iron-based hemoglobin appears red when oxygenated. Hemocyanin is less efficient but works better in cold, low-oxygen deep-sea environments. Octopuses have three hearts to compensate.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Animal biology. Corrected l1/l2. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors — all are plausible wrong chemical mechanisms for blue blood coloration.

q: Which exceptional regenerative abilities do axolotls possess throughout their entire adult lives?
a: Regenerating complete limbs, portions of the heart, and sections of the brain
d: Regenerating only skin and superficial wounds
Regenerating internal organs but not limbs
Regenerating the entire body from any fragment
Regenerating sensory organs (eyes and ears) only
Regenerating muscle tissue but not bone or nerve tissue
Regenerating the tail only, like many lizards
Regenerating fin tissue and gills only
e: Axolotls (Ambystoma mexicanum) retain juvenile traits including extraordinary regenerative capacity. Their cells can dedifferentiate to stem-cell-like states and then form the correct cell types for any missing structure — from limbs to heart and brain tissue. Scientists study this to understand human wound healing.
i: WRONG DOMAIN: Animal biology. Corrected l1/l2. Expanded from 3 to 8 distractors — all describe partial regenerative abilities that are wrong compared to the full capability.
